elements.section-2411-004: Energy Barrier Analysis for Minimum Coercivity
elements.sent-2411-150: In this section, we theoretically analyze the effect of a localized disturbance (in the form a needle domain) and residual stresses on magnetic hysteresis.
elements.sent-2411-151: Specifically, we evaluate the energy barriers limiting the growth of a needle domain on an otherwise uniformly magnetized ellipsoid, and correlate these barriers to the width of the hysteresis loop (or coercivity).
elements.sent-2411-152: By minimizing the energy barriers governing magnetization reversal, we arrive at a mathematical relationship between material constants and applied stresses for which magnetic hysteresis is minimized.
elements.sent-2411-153: Our calculation is by no means a rigorous theoretical proof, but an analytical interpretation of the relationship between material constants and mechanical loads that collectively lower magnetic hysteresis.
elements.sent-2411-154: We support our analysis using our numerical micromagnetic computations.
elements.sent-2411-155: As a first step, we analyze the stability of uniform magnetization on an ellipsoid under an external field.
elements.sent-2411-156: In the following sections, we build on this analysis by introducing a needle domain and applying residual stresses to the uniformly magnetized ellipsoid.
elements.sent-2411-157: We then analyze the combined effects of these localized disturbances and mechanical loads on energy barriers governing magnetization reversal.
elements.subsubsection-2411-001: Linear Stability Analysis of a Uniformly Magnetized Ellipsoid
elements.figure-2411-005: \begin{centering} \textcolor{black}{\includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{Figure5-Oct28-2024.png}} \caption{\label{TransientState}(a) Schematic illustration of the energy wells located at $\mathbf{m}_1 = \pm1$ and at $\mathbf{m}_2 = \pm1$. At these energy wells, the strains are minimized and correspond to the spontaneous strain values $\mathbf{E}_0 (\mathbf{m})$. (b) At the shoulder of the hysteresis loop (preceding magnetization reversal), a transient microstructural state is observed. In this state, the needle domain is extended to its maximum length and the domain walls are nearly parallel to one another. At this stage, the microstructure resembles a stripe-like domain with near-divergence-free magnetization. The ideal values of magnetization and strains in these domains correspond to the values at the energy wells, i.e., $\mathbf{m}=\mathrm{m}_1\hat{\mathbf{e}}_1$ and $\hat{\varepsilon}_{11} = 1, \hat{\varepsilon}_{22} = \hat{\varepsilon}_{33} = -\frac{1}{2}$, and $\mathbf{m}=\mathrm{m}_2\hat{\mathbf{e}}_2$ and $\hat{\varepsilon}_{22} = 1, \hat{\varepsilon}_{11} = \hat{\varepsilon}_{33} = -\frac{1}{2}$, respectively. For $i \neq j$ the shear strains are zero in both domains. (c) Illustration of a uniformly magnetized ellipsoid in linear stability analysis.} \par\end{centering}
elements.sent-2411-158: Let us consider a large oblate ellipsoid $\mathcal{E}$ in three-dimensional space $\mathbb{R ^3$ that is uniformly magnetized along the easy axis $\mathbf{m = \mathrm{m _1\hat{\mathbf{e _1$, see Fig.~\ref{TransientState (c).}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-159: In the absence of defects, the uniform magnetization minimizes the anisotropy energy (magnetization along easy axis), the demagnetization energy (with poles far apart on the ellipsoid), and the exchange energy (no domain walls).
elements.sent-2411-160: At mechanical equilibrium, the strains on the ellipsoid $\vb{E}$ are equal to the spontaneous values $\vb{E _0(\mathbf{m )$, and thus minimize the total elastic energy of the system.}}
elements.sent-2411-161: Under an external magnetic field $\mathbf{H _{\mathrm{ext = -\mathrm{H_{ext }\hat{\mathbf{e _1$, there is a finite Zeeman energy contribution in the system.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-162: This Zeeman energy is minimized when the magnetization on the ellipsoid reorients to align with the applied field.
elements.sent-2411-163: This reorientation, however, costs energy and perturbs the system away from equilibrium.
elements.sent-2411-164: To analyze this increase in the free energy, we model a small and uniform in-plane perturbation $\theta \to 0$ to rotate the magnetization away from its easy axis.
elements.sent-2411-165: That is, the magnetization is: \begin{equation}m =cos\theta~\hat{e}_1 + sin\theta~\hat{e}_2 \nonumber \approx \qty(1 - \dfrac{\theta^2}{2})\hat{e}_1 + \theta~\hat{e}_2 \quad for~\theta\ll 1. \label{magnetizationapprox}\end{equation} This perturbation contributes to an increase in the magnetocrystalline anisotropy energy, however, the uniform perturbation does not introduce magnetization gradients (no exchange energy).
elements.sent-2411-166: At mechanical equilibrium the elastic energy is negligible (because $\mathbf{E = \mathbf{E _0(\vb{m )$).}}}
elements.sent-2411-167: Recall that we consider a large plate-like ellipsoid with a surface normal along the the $\hat{\vb{e _3$ direction, see Fig.~\ref{TransientState (c).}}}

elements.sent-2411-170: At the coercivity point, the equilibrium and stability conditions are, respectively, given by: \begin{equation}\frac{\delta\Psi_\theta}{\delta\theta}=0, \quad \frac{\delta^2\Psi_\theta}{\delta\theta^2}\leq0.\end{equation}.

elements.subsection-2411-003: Effect of Residual Stress on the Energy Barrier
elements.sent-2411-220: We next analyze the effect of the applied or residual stresses on the energy barrier governing the growth of the needle domain.
elements.sent-2411-221: The total elastic energy stored in the ellipsoid under a constant applied or residual stress $\sigma_\mathrm{R}$ is: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \psi_{\mathrm{elas}}^{\mathrm{R}} = \int_{\mathcal{E}}\qty\Big{\dfrac{1}{2}[\mathbf{E}-\mathbf{E_{0}\mathrm{(\mathbf{m})}}]\cdot\mathbb{C}[\mathbf{E}-\mathbf{E_{0}\mathrm{(\mathbf{m})}}]-\sigma_\mathrm{R}\cdot\vb{E}}\dd\vb{x}. \end{aligned}\end{equation} Using $\mathbf{E _\mathrm{D = \mathbf{E -\mathbf{E _0\mathrm{(\mathbf{m )}$, and $\mathbb{S}$ as the compliance tensor, we rewrite the elastic energy as: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \psi_{\mathrm{elas}}^{\mathrm{R}} = \int_{\mathcal{E}}\qty\Big{\dfrac{1}{2}\vb{E}_{\mathrm{D}}\cdot\mathbb{C}\vb{E}_{\mathrm{D}}-\sigma_\mathrm{R}\cdot[\vb{E}_{\mathrm{D}}+\vb{E}_0(\vb{m})]}\dd\vb{x} = \int_{\mathcal{E}}\qty\Big{\dfrac{1}{2}\vb{E}_{\mathrm{D}}\cdot\mathbb{C}\vb{E}_{\mathrm{D}}+\dfrac{1}{2}\sigma_\mathrm{R}\cdot\mathbb{S}\sigma_\mathrm{R}-\dfrac{1}{2}\sigma_\mathrm{R}\cdot\mathbb{S}\sigma_\mathrm{R}-\sigma_\mathrm{R}\cdot[\vb{E}_{\mathrm{D}}+\vb{E}_0(\vb{m})]}\dd\vb{x} = \int_{\mathcal{E}}\qty[\dfrac{1}{2}(\vb{E}_{\mathrm{D}}-\mathbb{S}\sigma_\mathrm{R})\cdot\mathbb{C}(\vb{E}_{\mathrm{D}}-\mathbb{S}\sigma_\mathrm{R}) - \sigma_\mathrm{R}\cdot\vb{E}_0(\vb{m}) - \dfrac{1}{2}\sigma_\mathrm{R}\cdot\mathbb{S}\sigma_\mathrm{R}]\dd \vb{x} = \int_{\mathcal{E}}\qty[\dfrac{1}{2}\vb{E}_{\mathrm{eff}}\cdot\mathbb{C}\vb{E}_{\mathrm{eff}} - \sigma_\mathrm{R}\cdot\vb{E}_0(\vb{m})]\dd \vb{x} + \mathrm{Const.} \end{aligned} \label{psi_R}\end{equation} In Eq.~\ref{psi_R , the energy term $\int_\mathcal{E -\frac{1 {2}\sigma_{\mathrm{R }\cdot\mathbb{S}\sigma_{\mathrm{R }\mathrm{d}\mathbf{x}$ is a constant, and we define $\vb{E _{\mathrm{eff = \vb{E _{\mathrm{D - \mathbb{S}\sigma_\mathrm{R}$ to be an effective strain tensor under the applied or residual stress (see section~\ref{Strain tensor-supplement of the Supplement for strain decomposition).}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-247: In this work, we investigated whether and how magnetoelastic interactions, which are commonly neglected in the study of magnetic hysteresis, affect coercivity.
elements.sent-2411-248: Our theoretical analysis shows that magnetoelastic interactions, for suitable values of magnetostriction and applied stresses, dominate magnetization reversal and can be designed to reduce magnetic hysteresis.
elements.sent-2411-249: In Study 1, we show that the coercivity of an alloy can be reduced (i.e., comparable to permalloy) despite its large anisotropy constant at suitable values of magnetostriction constant $\lambda_{100}$ and residual stresses $\sigma_{\mathrm{R }$.}
elements.sent-2411-250: In Study 2, we use a theory-guided search of the material parameter space to establish coercivity maps as a function of magnetoelastic constants ($\lambda_{100 , c_{11 , c_{12}$) and applied stresses ($\sigma_{\mathrm{R }$).}}}
elements.sent-2411-251: These calculations establish coercivity heatmaps that provide quantitative guidelines to design magnets with small hysteresis.
elements.sent-2411-252: Finally, we theoretically analyze the energy barriers limiting the growth of a needle domain (under applied stresses) on magnetic hysteresis.
elements.sent-2411-253: Our results identify a parabolic relation between material constants and applied stresses $\kappa_1 = \alpha(c_{11 -c_{12 )(\lambda_{100 +\beta\sigma_{11 )^2$ for which coercivity is minimized.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-254: Below, we discuss some limiting conditions on our results and then highlight the key features of our work.
elements.sent-2411-255: In our micromagnetic calculations, we model a finite computational domain $\Omega$ embedded within an infinitely large ellipsoid $\mathcal{E}$.
elements.sent-2411-256: We numerically solve the governing equations Eq.~\ref{LLG --\ref{eq:mechanicaleq on $\Omega$ using a Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) algorithm, and analytically calculate the geometry-dependent demagnetization fields.}}
elements.sent-2411-257: The FFT introduces periodic boundary conditions on $\Omega$, which requires careful consideration of the computational domain to minimize stray field interactions between periodic images.
elements.sent-2411-258: To mitigate this effect, we select defect and domain sizes such that $V(\Omega_d) \ll V(\Omega)$, ensuring that the locally varying demagnetization field decays near computational domain boundaries, i.e., $\tilde{\mathbf{H _d \to \vb{0}$ (see \cite{balakrishna2021tool, renuka2024Rethinking for further details).}}}
elements.sent-2411-259: Another limitation in our study is the assumption of homogeneous and uniaxial applied stress when computating coercvity.
elements.sent-2411-260: In reality, applied or residual stresses---often localized and inhomogeneous, particularly near defects and precipitates---can accumulate during material synthesis or when integrating magnetic alloys into devices.
elements.sent-2411-261: These stress distributions, along with defect geometries and needle domain distribution, could affect our theoretical predictions of coercivity.
elements.sent-2411-262: Additionally, in our computations, we independently vary the magnetostriction constant $\lambda_{100}$ while keeping the elastic stiffness constants fixed.
elements.sent-2411-263: However, during alloy synthesis, altering the magnetostriction (e.g., via substitutional doping) may impact the elastic stiffness and vice versa.
elements.sent-2411-264: The interdependence between material constants is not fully understood, and a broader combinatorial investigation, using experimental validation, first-principles calculations, or model Hamiltonian approaches would be valuable.
elements.sent-2411-265: With these caveats in mind, we next highlight the key findings of our work.
elements.sent-2411-266: The distinguishing feature of our micromagnetics work is that we correlate magnetic coercivity with the energy barriers governing the growth of needle domains.
elements.sent-2411-267: In so doing, we capture the nuanced interplay between magnetocrystalline anisotropy and magnetostriction constants in lowering magnetic hysteresis.
elements.sent-2411-268: We find that the magnetization reverses readily in magnetic alloys with suitable magnetostrictive coefficients, despite their large anisotropy constants.
elements.sent-2411-269: This finding contrasts with some reports in the literature, in which the small magnetostriction (in addition to small anisotropy) is considered necessary for lowering hysteresis \cite{10.1063/1.369539,PhysRevB.66.174419,HERZER200599 .}
elements.sent-2411-270: While small magnetostriction and anisotropy constants contribute to small hysteresis and lowering these constants to near zero values has been the current strategy for developing soft magnetic alloys, as shown in Fig.~\ref{Introduction (a), our results expand the material parameter space to search for new combinations of magnetic constants (including $\lambda_{100 , \mathbb{C , \sigma_{\mathrm{R }$) with small hysteresis.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-271: Another feature of our work is that in addition to designing fundamental material constants (e.g., $\lambda_{100 , \mathbb{C}$) to reduce hysteresis, we show that one can introduce mechanical constraints, such as compressive or tensile stresses to tailor coercivity.}
elements.sent-2411-272: Furthermore, we show how applied or residual stresses affect the shape of the hysteresis loops, which would be relevant to the application of magnetic materials in engineering memory and actuator devices.
elements.sent-2411-273: Overall, we demonstrate how applied or residual stresses can be engineered to optimize the energy barriers governing magnetization reversal, and theoretically arrive a mathematical relation $\kappa_1 = \alpha(c_{11 -c_{12 )(\lambda_{100 +\beta\sigma_{11 )^2$ for minimum coercivity.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-274: This relation could serve as a quantitative design principle in the discovery of new soft magnetic alloys.
elements.sent-2411-275: To conclude, we present a micromagnetics framework based on magnetostriction and localized disturbances as a way forward to computing hysteresis in magnetic alloys.
elements.sent-2411-276: Using this approach, we show that magnetoelastic interactions, arising from magnetostriction, elastic stiffness constants, and applied or residual stresses, affect the shape and width of hysteresis loops.
elements.sent-2411-277: Our energy barrier analysis shows that the magnetoelastic interactions can be quantitatively designed following the $\kappa_1 = \alpha(c_{11 -c_{12 )(\lambda_{100 +\beta\sigma_{11 )^2$ design rule to reduce magnetic hysteresis despite large anisotropy constants.}}}}
